summary: Drew DeVault publicly announces that his long-teased 'secret project' is a new systems programming language (later revealed as Hare), under development since December 2019 with a target release in early 2022. The language features manual memory management, no runtime, a superset of the C ABI, and a compact 60-page specification. It aims to be a modernized alternative to C with improvements like tagged unions for error handling, memory-safe types, and Unicode-aware strings. DeVault emphasizes that the first public release will be production-ready, and he's seeking volunteers to help build out the standard library.
key_points: The language targets the same niche as C — compilers, OS utilities, network servers — but with modern improvements like tagged unions, memory-safe types, and Unicode support
It uses a superset of the C ABI for trivial interoperability with existing C libraries, prioritizing practical adoption
The specification is intentionally small at 60 pages, reflecting a philosophy of simplicity and a context-free, expression-oriented syntax
DeVault wants the first release to be complete and production-ready — no early preview, people should be able to ship working code immediately
The standard library mandate emphasizes concise interfaces, complete documentation, and sufficient testing as non-negotiable requirements
money_quotes: It has manual memory management, no runtime, and it uses a superset of the C ABI, making it easy to link with libraries and C code.
We have a mostly complete specification which clocks in at 60 pages.
The first time most people will hear about this language will also be the first time they can ship working code with it.
I plan to continue keeping the other details a secret until the release — we want the first release to be a complete, stable, production-ready programming language with all of the trimmings.
Have a concise and straightforward interface
Correctly and completely implement the useful subset of the required behavior
key_insight: A new systems language can succeed by staying small, targeting C's niche directly with modern improvements, and launching only when it's genuinely ready for production use.
